Love in the Time of Sickle Cell Disease, by Krithika Varagur

Harper's Magazine · by Krithika Varagur

Illustrations by Katherine Lam

Subomi Mabogunje fell for Nkechi Egonu within hours of meeting her in 2004, in his hometown of Ijebu-\xe2\x80\x89Ode, a trading hub in southwest Nigeria. They worked at a state-run broadcast TV station, thrown together by the National Youth Service Corps. He was speechless on the day Nkechi first walked into work. While Subomi was thin and bespectacled, she was petite and zaftig, with her hair in a ballerina bun, and coldly immune to the stares that trailed her across the office. Her swaggering personality was also the opposite of his reserved one; she was outspoken in the station\xe2\x80\x99s weekly news meeting, and top brass quickly promoted her to program presenter. She was the most exciting person, Subomi felt, who had ever walked into his hometown.

He found the courage to speak to Nkechi one weekend when they were assigned to do community service, clearing overgrown grasses near a government building. Subomi went, despite his habitual avoidance of strenuous physical activity. \xe2\x80\x9cYou\xe2\x80\x99re too good for this kind of work, ehe?\xe2\x80\x9d Nkechi teased, furrowing her brows. \xe2\x80\x9cLocals\xe2\x80\x99 discount,\xe2\x80\x9d he joked, and she laughed. With his hollow cheekbones, frail body, and elongated fingers, he was clearly what some uncharitable onlookers would call a \xe2\x80\x9csickler\xe2\x80\x9d\xe2\x80\x94\xe2\x80\x89one of up to six million people in Nigeria with sickle cell disease, a group of inherited blood disorders that turn red blood cells from soft discs into rigid crescents, frequently leading to blood clots that can cause pain episodes, called \xe2\x80\x9ccrises,\xe2\x80\x9d and serious complications in most major organs. But Nkechi never shied away from him. A few days later, the office\xe2\x80\x99s radio transmitter stopped working, so Nkechi and Subomi had nothing to do except talk. After work, they made a beeline for a local dive bar housed in a car wash. Within a few weeks, they were inseparable.

From the beginning, Nkechi knew that she and Subomi had \xe2\x80\x9cno business dating.\xe2\x80\x9d His genotype was SS: he had two abnormal S genes for hemoglobin, the oxygen-\xe2\x80\x89carrying protein in his blood. Nkechi\xe2\x80\x99s genotype was AS: she had one abnormal S gene and one normal A gene. Like an estimated quarter of all Nigerians, she was a silent carrier. There was a 50 percent chance that any child they had would suffer from sickle cell disease like their father. This was no light prospect. Subomi\xe2\x80\x99s own childhood had been marred by secrecy and shame over his condition. Nkechi, meanwhile, had lost four cousins to the disease. Those deaths might be understood as products of an earlier, benighted time, when the average Nigerian knew far less about genetic testing and disease management. Today, however, there was a growing consensus\xe2\x80\x94\xe2\x80\x89particularly in their college-\xe2\x80\x89educated, upper-\xe2\x80\x89middle-\xe2\x80\x89class milieu\xe2\x80\x94when it came to passing on two sickle cell genes: don\xe2\x80\x99t risk it.

But how, and when, do you weigh risk against attachment? Nkechi revealed her genotype just days after meeting Subomi, and he didn\xe2\x80\x99t stop loving her. He liked that she held her ground, didn\xe2\x80\x99t worry about the future, and could make even his stolid father laugh. She also had a quick temper, but he preferred her foot-\xe2\x80\x89stomping, door-\xe2\x80\x89slamming outbursts to his quieter family life, shuttling between two parents who often lived apart. \xe2\x80\x9cBut why, dude?\xe2\x80\x9d Nkechi asked him in those early months, in her broad, Americanized diphthongs. He wasn\xe2\x80\x99t sure, he said. She was something that had happened to him, like a coconut dropping on your head while you\xe2\x80\x99re walking down the street.

By the end of her service year, Nkechi had fallen in love with Subomi, too, but she thought it shouldn\xe2\x80\x99t last. In March 2005, she moved back to Lagos alone. Subomi simply followed her. In April 2006, she broke up with him at a fried chicken restaurant, screaming about how stupid, how uneducated, it would be for them to stay together. They reconciled a week later. She broke up with him again in January 2008, sitting in his parked car. That separation lasted a month.

Nkechi was just shy of thirty when she initiated their third breakup in late July 2009. She met him on a Friday evening at home and calmly told him that they had to find other people to marry before it was too late. As the oldest of six, she was starting to feel uneasy that neither she nor her siblings had married or started families, unusual for Nigerians of their generation. She had even talked to a genetic counselor at the Sickle Cell Foundation Nigeria (SCFN), in Lagos, who had advised her to end the relationship. (Though a representative from the foundation told me that this is against their policies.) There were too many obstacles. \xe2\x80\x9cA marriage is not just between two people,\xe2\x80\x9d Nkechi reminded Subomi. They talked and cried for three hours as the sun set, and she kept crying all the way home on the lurching bus. They were tears of grief, but also relief. She had finally, and responsibly, severed this unwieldiest of attachments.

Subomi didn\xe2\x80\x99t sleep much that night. Early the next morning, he started driving to his father\xe2\x80\x99s house in Ijebu-\xe2\x80\x89Ode. That afternoon, something told Nkechi to call him to make sure he had arrived safely. Subomi\xe2\x80\x99s boss picked up the phone.

\xe2\x80\x9cIs Subi there?\xe2\x80\x9d she asked, confused.

\xe2\x80\x9cOh, it\xe2\x80\x99s you, Nkechi. I was actually just about to call you,\xe2\x80\x9d Nkechi recalls him saying over a crackling connection. \xe2\x80\x9cSubomi\xe2\x80\x99s had an accident.\xe2\x80\x9d

Less than an hour outside Lagos, Subomi\xe2\x80\x99s sedan had been rear-\xe2\x80\x89ended, hit a truck, and flipped over. Paramedics had taken him to the hospital and dialed the last received call on the phone they fished from Subomi\xe2\x80\x99s pocket. Nkechi\xe2\x80\x99s mouth went dry. At dawn, she began a long bus journey to the hospital, where she ran into Subomi\xe2\x80\x99s mother. They found Subomi in the ICU, in a coma.

Nkechi held his mother\xe2\x80\x99s hand as the doctor explained Subomi\xe2\x80\x99s prognosis. It could be hours, days, weeks, or months before he woke up, if he woke up at all. Nkechi had to work on Monday, but she returned to Ibadan on Friday afternoon, inaugurating a regular weekend pilgrimage. She sat by Subomi\xe2\x80\x99s bed all day, napping at a nursing students\xe2\x80\x99 dorm nearby. Perhaps, his doctor offered, playing Subomi\xe2\x80\x99s favorite songs or movies could help. Nkechi made him a mixtape with his favorite songs by Kenny G, Enya, and the Nigerian gospel singer Tim Godfrey, which she had played on a loop day in and day out. His mother started to visibly relax whenever Nkechi walked through the hospital doors. Nkechi, for her part, put their breakup out of her mind.

On Saturday, September 5, she showed up as usual and was stunned to find Subomi awake. She screamed with joy and clasped his hands.

\xe2\x80\x9cHappy birthday, Nkechi,\xe2\x80\x9d he told her.

Everyone near his bed turned to her and paused. Was he crazy? But it was, in fact, her thirtieth birthday; Subomi had glanced at the date on some paperwork. Nkechi started to laugh. Everyone then agreed that he could go home soon, and he was discharged a few weeks later.

Subomi still faced a long road to recovery, and he was intermittently bedridden. Nkechi substituted her weekend trips to Ibadan with visits to his mother\xe2\x80\x99s house in Lagos. She guided him through complicated stretches, gave him sponge baths, and crawled on the floor beside him as he learned how to walk again. Remarkably, he was on his feet within six months\xe2\x80\x94\xe2\x80\x89and, despite everyone\xe2\x80\x99s concern, didn\xe2\x80\x99t suffer any sickle cell crises during his convalescence. When Nkechi got up to leave, he started to ask her when they were getting married.

\xe2\x80\x9cWe are broken up,\xe2\x80\x9d she would remind him.

\xe2\x80\x9cI have no recollection of that,\xe2\x80\x9d he would say. \xe2\x80\x9cOr did you forget I was in a monthlong coma?\xe2\x80\x9d It was hard for her to gauge his sincerity.

In the new year, Subomi could not only walk but drive again, and as he regained strength, the flame of Nkechi\xe2\x80\x99s attraction flickered. She didn\xe2\x80\x99t try to put it out. For once, none of their family members were asking probing questions about the future. One afternoon in August, when Nkechi was making sure Subomi\xe2\x80\x99s bed was comfortable, he pulled a pink tourmaline ring from his pocket.

Later, Nkechi\xe2\x80\x99s mind would cycle through the past six years\xe2\x80\x94\xe2\x80\x89their chance meeting in Ijebu-\xe2\x80\x89Ode, those afternoons at the car-wash bar, breaking and making up in Lagos, the accident, his convalescence\xe2\x80\x94\xe2\x80\x89and she would wonder if they were making the right choice. But in that moment, and without hesitation, she said yes.

Nigeria is the sickle cell capital of the world. Its residents account for about half of all new annual cases of severe hemoglobin disorders worldwide. Sickle cell disease is one of the world\xe2\x80\x99s most prevalent autosomal recessive genetic disorders; the sickle cell trait is over six times more common in Nigerians than the cystic fibrosis gene is among people of Northern European descent, or the Tay-\xe2\x80\x89Sachs gene among Ashkenazi Jews.

In the Fifties, a number of scientists speculated that the sickle cell trait confers some resistance to malaria\xe2\x80\x89\xe2\x80\x94now a widely accepted theory\xe2\x80\x94\xe2\x80\x89which would account for the prevalence of the gene in sub-\xe2\x80\x89Saharan Africa, home to over 90 percent of all malaria cases in the world. Over millennia, per this hypothesis, as more AS than AA children survived acute malaria infections and reached reproductive age, they passed on their single S genes, too. But for those with two such genes, the potential complications include acute pain episodes, acute chest syndrome, strokes, priapism, jaundice, numb chin syndrome, an enlarged spleen, leg ulcers, and damaged blood cells in the retina, which can lead to blindness.

The heritability of sickle cell anemia, the most common and severe form of SCD, is about as straightforward as a Punnett square from a middle school biology textbook: if both parents are carriers (AS/AS), their children have a 25 percent chance of having the disease. If one parent has the disease (SS) and the other has no sickle cell genes (AA), there\xe2\x80\x99s a 100 percent chance their kids will be carriers (AS) and a 0 percent chance they will have the disease. \xe2\x80\x89A couple like Nkechi and Subomi, where one parent has the disease and one is a carrier, has a 50 percent chance of giving birth to an SS child and a 50 percent chance of an AS child. Every year, a growing number of people in Nigeria consider breaking up because of this calculation.

Dr. Olufemi Akinyanju, an eighty-\xe2\x80\x89six-\xe2\x80\x89year-\xe2\x80\x89old Nigerian hematologist, started identifying sickle cell patients as a young doctor in Lagos in the Sixties, fresh out of medical school in London. The patients had often never heard of the disease, and he went on to spend most of his career studying it. In 1994, he founded the Sickle Cell Foundation Nigeria in Lagos, now one of the region\xe2\x80\x99s preeminent treatment and diagnosis centers. His generation of doctors helped SCD testing take root in Nigeria. The most common blood test is hemoglobin electrophoresis, in which electrical currents are passed through a blood sample, separating different types of hemoglobin into discrete bands. The test is now commonplace in urban centers like Lagos and Abuja. Testing in a high-\xe2\x80\x89end facility can cost up to forty dollars, but there are also free and cheap clinics, which can provide results in as little as thirty minutes.

More and more Nigerians are now being encouraged to test at an early age. Nkechi, who was born in 1979, has known her genotype since she was seven. Testing is less accessible outside major cities, especially in the country\xe2\x80\x99s poorer northern states. And in lower-\xe2\x80\x89end clinics, results are often inaccurate. Ezekiel Ogbu, a thirty-\xe2\x80\x89six-\xe2\x80\x89year-\xe2\x80\x89old bus driver with SCD in Lagos, found this out the hard way in 2018, when he discovered that his fianc\xc3\xa9e was not AA, as she had long believed, but AS. They broke up two weeks before their wedding, following her third blood test, and he informed more than five hundred guests that the ceremony was off. Many Nigerian sickle cell activists now recommend two or three different blood tests to be sure.

Determining your genotype is one thing, but puzzling out its implications is another. Absent strict or uniform laws, decisions about dating often fall to individuals. Genotype screening is now a requirement to get married in many Nigerian churches and mosques, but some young people are starting higher upstream\xe2\x80\x89\xe2\x80\x94as early as the first date. Given Nigeria\xe2\x80\x99s emphasis on marriage, combined with its relatively low incidence of prenatal testing and the illegality of abortion in most cases, a social norm is rapidly consolidating to dissuade two people with sickle cell genes from marrying, or even dating. But not everyone makes this choice. A 2015 study found that one in five people who realized they were in AS-AS relationships from their premarital screenings got married anyway.

Some lawmakers have tried to intervene in this gray area. States in both the north and the south have passed measures to mandate premarital testing; in 2020, the Nigerian Senate debated an SCD management bill that, among other things, would also encourage premarital tests. During these discussions, Chukwuka Utazi, a senator from Enugu State, implied that he had suffered a genotype-\xe2\x80\x89related breakup of his own. He understood what a painful choice it could be. But in Africa, he maintained, \xe2\x80\x9cwe marry for children, we don\xe2\x80\x99t marry for love.\xe2\x80\x9d (Nigeria already polices the intimate sphere: public displays of same-\xe2\x80\x89sex affection are illegal, for example, and punishment for same-sex intercourse can range from prison time to execution by stoning.) Another senator chimed in: \xe2\x80\x9cWe will not allow love to take away the best part of our marriages.\xe2\x80\x9d

Heterosexual Nigerian couples who come to these genetic crossroads are not merely grappling with whether to break up or get married. They are also considering what makes a good life, both for themselves and for their prospective children. SCD is not a death sentence\xe2\x80\x94\xe2\x80\x89especially in a city like Lagos\xe2\x80\x94\xe2\x80\x89but it is usually a life sentence. Its two most effective treatments, bone marrow transplants and gene editing, are out of reach for ordinary people. Most Nigerians with SCD must deal with painful crises, procure expensive medications, seek out specialists, and confront stigma, including obstacles to someday starting a family of their own. Should such people come into existence at all? The question sounds crude or even amoral, but when Nigerian carrier couples split up, their implicit answer is no.

Their concerns are broadly relevant to the rest of us who live in the age of genetic testing and its novel theaters of decision-\xe2\x80\x89making. Prospective parents find themselves asking: Which diseases should we test for? How risky is it to pass on a certain trait? Which conditions truly compromise a child\xe2\x80\x99s future, and which are ultimately manageable? How much can you really control? And what is worth sacrificing to be with the person you love? As genetic testing continues to spread, many more people, in many more places, will try to answer these questions. In Nigeria, they are already part of daily life.

Perched on the Bight of Benin, Lagos is home to some fifteen million people and is the center of Africa\xe2\x80\x99s largest economy. The stretch marks of the developing world are visible everywhere: cheap cell phone data sold at hand-\xe2\x80\x89painted stalls, piles of refuse, strip malls that pop up overnight like mushrooms, awe-\xe2\x80\x89inspiring traffic, and unflinching sales-\xe2\x80\x89children who dash through it. The city abuts the Lagos Lagoon, where a vast slum is held up on stilts and fishermen still use dugout canoes to catch red snapper and mackerel. \xe2\x80\x9cSometimes,\xe2\x80\x9d as the Nigerian playwright Wole Soyinka once wrote, \xe2\x80\x9cone feels that the world\xe2\x80\x99s discards, the detritus of the constantly surging ocean, eventually come to rest on the beaches of Lagos.\xe2\x80\x9d

Last year, I made several trips to this amphibious megacity to meet Nigerians who have navigated dating, love, and marriage under the star of their sickle cell genotype. It\xe2\x80\x99s one of the likeliest places in Nigeria to have a few partners before marriage; it is also where the greatest number of Nigerians go to work. Like all cities, it\xe2\x80\x99s filled with strivers trying to make rational decisions about their future; a 2021 study of over 1,300 city-\xe2\x80\x89dwelling Nigerians found that 29 percent of respondents had ended a relationship due to \xe2\x80\x9cgenotype incompatibility.\xe2\x80\x9d In this city rife with choice and its discontents, there may be more than 3.5 million people with the sickle cell trait.

Among the Lagosians I met were: several individuals who had broken up with their carrier boyfriend or girlfriend in their twenties; a forty-\xe2\x80\x89something couple who, upon discovering their daughter was SS, belatedly realized they both had AS genotypes; a single mother of three whose husband left her after two of their SS children started having severe crises; several older single women with the sickle cell trait who had given up on marriage altogether; and a woman in her seventies with SCD who had been happily married for over fifty years. I heard about people who had forged their genotype test results, people who hid their status from their spouses, and couples who lied to get married in their house of worship. People explained their weighty decisions to me straightforwardly, as if recalling what they had for lunch. Everyone I spoke to was religious\xe2\x80\x94\xe2\x80\x89either Christian or Muslim, Nigeria\xe2\x80\x99s two major faiths\xe2\x80\x94\xe2\x80\x89and expressed the belief that any outcome, be it heartbreak, late-\xe2\x80\x89in-\xe2\x80\x89life romance, or a child with surprise SCD, had, to some degree, been willed by God.

It\xe2\x80\x99s not that young Lagosians are pure fatalists or cold-\xe2\x80\x89blooded optimizers; some do allow themselves genetically risky attachments, even if they\xe2\x80\x99re not meant to last. I met Blessing Assam, a twenty-\xe2\x80\x89five-\xe2\x80\x89year-\xe2\x80\x89old pastor\xe2\x80\x99s daughter and accounting student with dreams of running a large business, who has an AS genotype. Blessing has worked a series of tough jobs to put herself through university, including serving as a live-\xe2\x80\x89in care worker for a woman with severe SCD. She has been dating Samson, a handsome twenty-\xe2\x80\x89nine-\xe2\x80\x89year-\xe2\x80\x89old entrepreneur, who also has an AS genotype, for two years. They video chat every night; he speaks to her in a soft voice and lets her order anything she wants whenever they go out without glancing at the price. But both told me they will have to break up. Blessing saw severe SCD at work, and three of Samson\xe2\x80\x99s family members have died of the disease. They don\xe2\x80\x99t want to risk it. \xe2\x80\x9cI need to wake up from my dreamworld,\xe2\x80\x9d she told me over lunch. She made a noose gesture and cocked her head to one side.

Other Lagosians who have SCD themselves dream of a relationship as an escape to a better life. Princess Samuel, a thirty-\xe2\x80\x89year-\xe2\x80\x89old with cut-\xe2\x80\x89glass cheekbones and a silvery voice, grew up sharing her church\xe2\x80\x99s spare room with her mother and younger siblings after her father\xe2\x80\x99s death. Her family could barely afford mild painkillers, but she stoically endured frequent vaso-\xe2\x80\x89occlusive crises, which happen when sickled red blood cells congeal and deprive tissues of oxygen. At university, she met and fell in love with a medical student who promised he didn\xe2\x80\x99t care about her genotype. For three years, they kept her status a secret from his less tolerant parents. Then, in 2017, she had an acute crisis and was rushed to the hospital. Her boyfriend feared for her life and called in favors to fast-\xe2\x80\x89track her treatment\xe2\x80\x94\xe2\x80\x89including with his mom, who was a nurse at the hospital where Princess was admitted. She helped Princess get priority treatment, and then promptly demanded that they break up. They did, and he married someone else last October.

Princess has had a few flirtations since then, but all have faded when she has been sick or hospitalized. Last spring, she quit her job and moved back in with her mother, who lives in a cinder-\xe2\x80\x89block house on the city\xe2\x80\x99s outskirts. Every Sunday, Princess wakes up as early as 4:30 am to commute to The Elevation Church in Ikorodu, which has a dedicated service for unmarried congregants. Last year, she had three more vaso-\xe2\x80\x89occlusive crises. The most serious one, in March, left her with a hospital bill that cost nearly as much as her mother\xe2\x80\x99s yearly rent. SCD is expensive, both acutely and chronically: prevention regimens of vitamins, painkillers, and antibiotics (up to $30 a month, for Princess), hospital admissions (sometimes $150 per day at a private hospital), blood transfusions (often over $100). These all hit harder given Nigeria\xe2\x80\x99s flailing economy and rapidly weakening currency. That\xe2\x80\x99s not just for patients, but also for their prospective partners and in-laws. \xe2\x80\x9cPeople think the cheapest is just to walk away, really,\xe2\x80\x9d Timi Edwin, a thirty-\xe2\x80\x89five-\xe2\x80\x89year-\xe2\x80\x89old SCD advocate who has the disease, told me. She speaks from experience: Timi\xe2\x80\x99s fianc\xc3\xa9 left her six years ago because of her condition.

Princess\xe2\x80\x99s mother is still optimistic. Sure, she says, it\xe2\x80\x99s a little harder for people with SCD to marry\xe2\x80\x94\xe2\x80\x89but maybe Princess should also try a little harder to meet single men. \xe2\x80\x9cThey are marrying,\xe2\x80\x9d she mused, about sickle cell patients she\xe2\x80\x99s met through her church. \xe2\x80\x9cSome even to wealthy people who are able to take care of them.\xe2\x80\x9d She went on: \xe2\x80\x9cI believe God will give her a wealthy husband too,\xe2\x80\x9d she said. \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99m praying seriously toward it.\xe2\x80\x9d

Nkechi and Subomi got married twice: first in an Igbo wedding in her family\xe2\x80\x99s village in Mbaise, in the same house where her parents married, and then in a Yoruba ceremony in Ijebu-\xe2\x80\x89Ode. The Igbo wedding was on December 3, 2010, and Nkechi turned out, against expectations, to be a calm and happy bride. She wore a lilac crepe wrapper, tied like a skirt, and a coral-\xe2\x80\x89bead headdress. Subomi wore a shirt printed with roaring lions, a traditional Igbo motif. A week later, in Ijebu-\xe2\x80\x89Ode, Subomi\xe2\x80\x99s cousin washed Nkechi\xe2\x80\x99s feet as she stepped in, as a bride, to a house that she already knew so well. They skipped a third church wedding, which also let them sidestep premarital genotype testing requirements.

Nkechi\xe2\x80\x99s sister Uche met Subomi several times when they dated, and found him strikingly thoughtful. Once, when he couldn\xe2\x80\x99t make it to a lunch they had planned in Lagos, he mailed her a check so she could take herself out on her intern\xe2\x80\x99s salary. \xe2\x80\x9cMy entire family\xe2\x80\x99s only concern, really, was the genotype,\xe2\x80\x9d Uche told me. \xe2\x80\x9cI asked her several times, are you ready\xe2\x80\x94\xe2\x80\x89really ready\xe2\x80\x94\xe2\x80\x89if you have an SS child?\xe2\x80\x9d And was she very sure his family knew she was a carrier too? \xe2\x80\x9cBut then she did a fantastic job taking care of him after the accident,\xe2\x80\x9d Uche said. It felt silly to keep probing. \xe2\x80\x9cWhereas an accident like that, for me, would have been a time to reevaluate things,\xe2\x80\x9d said Chijioke Nwamara, a childhood friend, \xe2\x80\x9cfor Nkechi it just strengthened her resolve to press ahead.\xe2\x80\x9d

As a wedding present, Subomi\xe2\x80\x99s parents gave them a two-\xe2\x80\x89bedroom house in their Lagos compound. Both families were in high spirits through Christmas, including Nkechi\xe2\x80\x99s one-\xe2\x80\x89hundred-\xe2\x80\x89year-\xe2\x80\x89old grandmother, who was overjoyed that her grandchild was finally embarking on her adult life. Nkechi and Subomi didn\xe2\x80\x99t tell her, or anyone else, that they had decided, as a condition of staying together, not to have children.

As newlyweds, they could finally live together, and they loved their city. They spent weekends at Lagos\xe2\x80\x99s nightclubs and urban beaches, and went to separate churches on Sunday mornings. Sometimes, they collaborated on graphic design work. They were modern, unconventional, independent; colleagues, freelancers, best friends. But things started to change around their first anniversary. More nights were spent staying home to watch TV than out on the town. Subomi, who had grown up lonely, started to feel lonely again, like there weren\xe2\x80\x99t enough people at home. He began twisting Nkechi\xe2\x80\x99s arm: What if they tried for a baby? \xe2\x80\x9cThere\xe2\x80\x99s got to be more to life than this,\xe2\x80\x9d he told her. \xe2\x80\x9cPlease? Just one?\xe2\x80\x9d

They found themselves attending their friends\xe2\x80\x99 babies\xe2\x80\x99 naming ceremonies and birthdays, and then Nkechi\xe2\x80\x99s younger brothers started having children, too. Her resolve to remain child-\xe2\x80\x89free gradually waned. She struck up tentative conversations with doctors, who told her that the science of SCD management had advanced in leaps and bounds, and that babies born with an SS genotype could live to old age. At thirty-\xe2\x80\x89two, Nkechi decided to stop taking her birth control pill. Looking back, she thinks she was inviting fate to intervene in a battle of the wills between two awfully stubborn people.

Nothing happened for four years. Nkechi knew she was on the older side for pregnancy, but she started to avoid the subject with Subomi, who could slip into a dark mood when he ruminated on his unfulfilled dreams of fatherhood. But in early 2016, after days of a sore throat and nausea, a pharmacist forced Nkechi to take a pregnancy test. It was positive. When she told Subomi, he burst into tears.

But they had to think of the big question in the same breath. Testing the fetus\xe2\x80\x99s sickle cell genotype would require an invasive test at the Sickle Cell Foundation Nigeria, and Subomi begged her not to do it, both because the procedure posed a small risk of miscarriage and because it had taken them so long to conceive. Nkechi called family members on both sides for advice, and she remembers many of them saying the same thing: there had been vast improvements in SCD management, and anyway, a 50 percent chance was far from a guarantee. A week passed as Nkechi mulled it over, during which time the fetus started to kick, and she realized she could not go through with an illicit abortion, whatever the genotype was. \xe2\x80\x9cWell,\xe2\x80\x9d she told Subomi, \xe2\x80\x9chere we go. What\xe2\x80\x99s the worst that could happen?\xe2\x80\x9d

Nkechi went into labor the night of Donald Trump and Hillary Clinton\xe2\x80\x99s last presidential debate, which she and Subomi watched in their living room. When the sun came up on October 20, he drove her to the hospital. By noon, she was admitted for a cesarean section. The baby had nestled close to her rib cage and had to be massaged out by two nurses. Nkechi woke up from the anesthesia in a cold sweat and learned that her baby\xe2\x80\x94\xe2\x80\x89her son\xe2\x80\x94\xe2\x80\x89was in another room, where nurses were starting him on a ventilator after he had ingested his own stool on the way out. When Nkechi saw him for the first time, he was on intravenous antibiotics. She didn\xe2\x80\x99t expect to feel so attached to this small person\xe2\x80\x89\xe2\x80\x94so responsible for him. They brought him home two days later. The name Subomi had picked out for him was Oluwagbotemi, Yoruba for \xe2\x80\x9cGod heard my cry.\xe2\x80\x9d But given the complicated birth, he decided to revise his son\xe2\x80\x99s name to Momoreoluwa, meaning \xe2\x80\x9cI know the grace of God.\xe2\x80\x9d Momo, for short.

Subomi was overjoyed. He spent all day with Momo, rambling to him about current events, changing his diapers, bathing him, and watching him sleep. He started reciting Yoruba phrases well before Momo could talk, so he\xe2\x80\x99d become worthy of his florid name. He took to calling him his \xe2\x80\x9cmini me.\xe2\x80\x9d But Subomi was also on edge until they brought three-\xe2\x80\x89month-\xe2\x80\x89old Momo for a sickle cell screening. When it indicated that he was just a carrier, they were walking on air.

Seven months later, Momo woke up sobbing, with swollen hands. It was, as Nkechi\xe2\x80\x99s mother quickly recognized, dactylitis: swelling of the fingers or toes due to blocked blood vessels\xe2\x80\x94\xe2\x80\x89it\xe2\x80\x99s a classic early symptom of SCD. But what about the genotype test? His fetal hemoglobin, which lingers for months, may have confused the results. His parents had been advised to bring him in for another test on his first birthday, but they had hoped it would be perfunctory. Nkechi bundled Momo into their car and drove to the hospital where he\xe2\x80\x99d been born. A new blood test confirmed that he was SS. That night, he started his daily program of antibiotics and antimalarial pills.

Subomi was too distressed to be of much help. Part of his grief, Nkechi knew, came from the painful memories of his own childhood, when distant relatives told him he\xe2\x80\x99d die before he was ten, and his parents, wary of stigma, warned him to avoid mentioning his disease. He used to sit miserably on the sidelines at soccer games and take countless days off from school. But Momo\xe2\x80\x99s case was different from Subomi\xe2\x80\x99s. He had been diagnosed early, life expectancies for patients had risen, and his parents knew much more about managing the disease. Subomi had never developed a solid regimen of preventive medication as a child; Momo was started on one before his first birthday. And Nkechi was going to be open about her son\xe2\x80\x99s condition, not hide it. \xe2\x80\x9cYou can do better for Momo than your parents did for you,\xe2\x80\x9d she told Subomi. They were both anxious after Momo\xe2\x80\x99s diagnosis, but after months of keeping infections at bay, they relaxed slightly. They even felt cautiously optimistic. Maybe they hadn\xe2\x80\x99t been totally reckless, after all, in rolling the genetic dice. Maybe the worst that could happen wasn\xe2\x80\x99t that bad.

The age of relatively cheap genetic testing has created a new term for those in Nkechi and Subomi\xe2\x80\x99s situation: \xe2\x80\x9cgenetically at risk.\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\x9cGenetic risk does not imply resignation in the face of an implacable biological destiny,\xe2\x80\x9d wrote the sociologists Carlos Novas and Nikolas Rose in 2000; \xe2\x80\x9cit induces new and active relations to oneself and one\xe2\x80\x99s future.\xe2\x80\x9d Such thought also \xe2\x80\x9creshapes prudence and obligation,\xe2\x80\x9d because \xe2\x80\x89those who are genetically at risk must make the right choices, both for their own sakes and for those of their families. These actors try to exercise \xe2\x80\x9cgenetic responsibility,\xe2\x80\x9d a concept coined in 1974 by the medical researchers Peter T. Rowley and Mack Lipkin Jr. They argued that the nascent field of genetics would compel more and more individuals to act responsibly, especially by avoiding the spread of genetic diseases. While the science may be new, they wrote, the problem is not. Whether to suffer or to not live at all \xe2\x80\x9chas been debated since Job.\xe2\x80\x9d

There are not yet universal standards for genetic testing, but a narrow consensus has emerged around specific conditions. The first prenatal diagnosis for Down syndrome was made in 1968. Since then, screenings have become routine for pregnant women in many parts of the world. In Iceland, for instance, the vast majority of women choose to be screened, and up to 85 percent of those who learn of an increased likelihood of the disorder choose to terminate the pregnancy. This shared idea of genetic responsibility has resulted, locally, in the condition\xe2\x80\x99s virtual disappearance, though a few babies with Down syndrome are still born there each year. But the polarized debates around the practice show that genetic responsibility is far from a neutral, or standardized, concept. The situation in Iceland has drawn global ire, and some have argued that these abortions offer a \xe2\x80\x9cbackdoor to eugenics.\xe2\x80\x9d

But people in societies where abortion has been illegal or discouraged have also created local norms, in which decisions are made earlier in the process of family formation, and often more communally. That was precisely the case with beta-\xe2\x80\x89thalassemia, another recessive genetic blood disorder, in Cyprus. In the Sixties, up to eighty babies with the condition were born there each year, and in the Seventies, local physicians began to strongly encourage carrier screening, counseling, and prenatal testing, eventually pressuring the Cypriot Orthodox Church to request premarital screening certificates. In 1986, the number of babies born with beta-\xe2\x80\x89thalassemia dropped to zero. Tay-\xe2\x80\x89Sachs disease is another example. In 1983, an ultra-\xe2\x80\x89Orthodox Brooklyn rabbi who lost four young children to Tay-\xe2\x80\x89Sachs\xe2\x80\x94\xe2\x80\x89which causes damage to nerve cells and leads most patients to die before age five\xe2\x80\x94\xe2\x80\x89started Dor Yeshorim. The organization, whose name is Hebrew for \xe2\x80\x9cupright generation,\xe2\x80\x9d is a premarital screening service that has rapidly become central to matchmaking in many ultra-\xe2\x80\x89Orthodox communities. Today, Dor Yeshorim representatives draw blood samples from Orthodox students, test them for several genetic diseases, and store the results in a confidential database. Early in a prospective match, the couple can call Dor Yeshorim, and if the individuals are both carriers for the same disease, they are simply told that the match is not advisable.

Both of these examples were enacted in relatively small, homogenous communities. But it\xe2\x80\x99s hard to closely follow either playbook in a massive, multiethnic democracy like Nigeria. Instead, over the past half-\xe2\x80\x89century, Nigerians have forged their own notions of genetic responsibility.

In 1986, Dr. Akinyanju led the first trainings of genetic counselors in Lagos. In the beginning, they worked primarily with families who already had SS children, but, by the Nineties, they started to see far more \xe2\x80\x9cprospective couples,\xe2\x80\x9d according to Ayo Otaigbe, who was among Nigeria\xe2\x80\x99s first class of trained genetic counselors. Over five hundred SCFN-\xe2\x80\x89trained counselors now work in a number of African countries where SCD is prevalent, from Ghana to Tanzania, and they are the most professionalized tip of an iceberg that includes public health campaigns, widespread testing, and rising awareness of SCD in popular culture. (\xe2\x80\x9cSickle cell been show me crises,\xe2\x80\x9d the Afropop singer Adekunle Gold sang in last year\xe2\x80\x99s single \xe2\x80\x9c5 Star,\xe2\x80\x9d which he has remixed with Rick Ross. \xe2\x80\x9cI will never forget the Nineties\xe2\x80\x89/\xe2\x80\x89Many nights I go dey beg for mercy.\xe2\x80\x9d) The watchword of SCFN-\xe2\x80\x89trained counselors is \xe2\x80\x9cnon-\xe2\x80\x89directive\xe2\x80\x9d: they provide information but do not compel specific action, and they especially avoid discouraging marriage or procreation. This fits with the global rise of non-\xe2\x80\x89directive genetic counseling in the Fifties, which emphasized voluntary measures in the shadow of Nazi atrocities. The manual used by SCFN to train counselors advises them to avoid phrases like \xe2\x80\x9cif I were you\xe2\x80\x9d and \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99m terribly sorry for you.\xe2\x80\x9d

Sickle cell counseling never took off in the same way in the United States, even though about one in thirteen black babies are born with the sickle cell trait. In 1972, Congress passed an act that encouraged voluntary sickle cell genotype testing among black Americans, and sickle cell screening was even a part of the Black Panther Party\xe2\x80\x99s public health initiatives. But \xe2\x80\x9cpeople started hollering \xe2\x80\x98genocide\xe2\x80\x99 pretty fast,\xe2\x80\x9d recalls Dr. Wally Smith, who now directs Virginia Commonwealth University\xe2\x80\x99s SCD program. He says the idea of a public health program directed at black Americans was hampered by widespread distrust of the government and medical authorities. Smith believes they might have given up too soon, lamenting a 2017 poll in which only 36 percent of the black Americans surveyed even knew that they had an elevated risk of SCD.

Nigerian experts are split on the future of SCD in their country. In just a couple generations, awareness has skyrocketed and free clinics have proliferated. Though statistics are scarce, those working in the field believe the number of cases has fallen, especially in urban centers. But the uptake of testing and shift in social norms has been polarized along socioeconomic lines. Fewer children in middle-\xe2\x80\x89 and upper-\xe2\x80\x89class homes seem to be born SS, observed Toyin Adesola, the executive director of an SCD advocacy organization. That means the disease is slowly concentrating in poorer communities. In Kano State, part of the less-\xe2\x80\x89developed, Muslim-\xe2\x80\x89majority north, prevalence of the sickle cell trait has been reported to be as high as 41 percent, compared with about 25 percent nationwide.

\xe2\x80\x9cWe don\xe2\x80\x99t see eradication as the goal, at least for now,\xe2\x80\x9d said Dr. Annette Akinsete, the national director of the Sickle Cell Foundation. And she does not think laws to stop carrier unions will ever find a foothold. Instead, she hopes to see more widespread newborn screening so that patients can get a head start managing the condition, as well as free or subsidized antibiotics and supplements.

In high-\xe2\x80\x89income countries, the median survival age for a patient with SCD has risen to between forty and sixty\xe2\x80\x94\xe2\x80\x89more than double what it was in the Seventies. In such places, SCD has evolved from a life-\xe2\x80\x89threatening pediatric condition to a chronic disease among adults. The average survival age in Nigeria is much lower\xe2\x80\x94\xe2\x80\x89likely under five, though statistics are not widely available\xe2\x80\x94\xe2\x80\x89because of the high infant mortality rates in poor regions. But a middle-\xe2\x80\x89class Nigerian patient with access to resources, especially in Lagos, has a \xe2\x80\x9cvery good chance\xe2\x80\x9d of managing their condition into adulthood, says Dr. Akinsete. Most Nigerian medical professionals don\xe2\x80\x99t see their goal as zero future SCD cases, but somewhat fewer of them, and a much higher quality of life for those born with the condition.

In the United States today, pregnant women can find out whether their unborn child has cystic fibrosis, fragile X syndrome, spinal muscular atrophy, or many other conditions. Prospective parents can quickly determine their carrier status for hundreds of disorders, from primary congenital glaucoma to maple syrup urine disease to a bone disease that increases the likelihood of fractures. People with no thoughts of parenthood, too, can get a basic genetic profile in a matter of weeks; for about two hundred dollars,\xe2\x80\x8923andMe can detect one\xe2\x80\x99s carrier status for over two dozen conditions\xe2\x80\x94\xe2\x80\x89and also estimate the likelihood of someday developing breast cancer or Alzheimer\xe2\x80\x99s. Without public health initiatives, it\xe2\x80\x99s unlikely that these advancements will dramatically reduce the prevalence of any particular condition. They will, however, put more people in positions like that of Nkechi and Subomi: having genetic information in the back of their minds while dating, falling in love, and starting a family\xe2\x80\x94\xe2\x80\x89and deciding whether they\xe2\x80\x99re willing to intervene on the basis of that information.

\xe2\x80\x9cWhen science moves faster than moral understanding, as it does today, men and women struggle to articulate their unease,\xe2\x80\x9d the political philosopher Michael J. Sandel has written on parenting after the genomic revolution. \xe2\x80\x9cIn liberal societies, they reach first for the language of autonomy, fairness, and individual rights.\xe2\x80\x9d But this framework quickly reveals its limits. In developing and developed countries alike, many families, engaging in the most optimistic of enterprises, eventually reach a point where meaningful choices evaporate, confronting all manner of shortcomings both before and after birth\xe2\x80\x94\xe2\x80\x89those stemming from health outcomes, but also those of resources, community, and chance. In the age of genetic testing, more information does not always mean fewer obstacles; conversely, it may entrench parents\xe2\x80\x99 struggle for control. Discrete decisions tend to give way, as Sandel puts it, to \xe2\x80\x9cquestions about the moral status of nature, and about the proper stance of human beings toward the given world.\xe2\x80\x9d

Momo grew the same whorled hair on his head that Nkechi has on her arms, but he had Subomi\xe2\x80\x99s dark complexion, slim feet, dark lashes, and occasional indignant frown. He refused to walk until he was two, and then quickly proceeded to somersaults. And he refused to eat solid food unless Nkechi took off her shirt, as if she were about to breastfeed him; only then could she shovel forkfuls of rice into his mouth, drawing peals of laughter from Subomi. Momo cheerfully moved from lap to lap at church and was sanguine when Nkechi left him with his aunt, who disciplined him alongside her own children. He turned two without another crisis. Nkechi decided to bring him to Mbaise for Christmas in 2018. Subomi stayed in Lagos to celebrate with his mom and sister.

When Nkechi got home, she noticed something on Subomi\xe2\x80\x99s leg as they got ready for bed: a yellowing ulcer, the size of a grape, over his left ankle. \xe2\x80\x9cCome on, man,\xe2\x80\x9d she said. \xe2\x80\x9cI leave you for a week and this happens?\xe2\x80\x9d It was nothing to worry about, he protested. It didn\xe2\x80\x99t even hurt. Leg ulcers are relatively common in adults with SCD. Subomi resisted going to a doctor, and Nkechi hoped it would resolve itself. Instead, it expanded to the width of a lime. Then Subomi\xe2\x80\x99s feet started to swell, and in February, a pinprick-\xe2\x80\x89size ulcer opened up over his right ankle. It grew into an equally large crater by Valentine\xe2\x80\x99s Day, when they could not make it out of the house for dinner.

On February 21, Subomi woke up at around a quarter to six, screaming in pain. A friend drove him to the hospital, where he was immediately admitted to the emergency ward. Nkechi was relieved; she had won the battle of convincing Subomi to see a doctor at all. Momo was in high spirits when he waved goodbye to his dad.

When Nkechi arrived an hour later, doctors had started Subomi on antibiotics and dressed his wounds. He told her that he would be discharged in a few days. It could have been much worse, said the doctor, and they had brought him in at the right time. \xe2\x80\x9cWhy didn\xe2\x80\x99t you bring me strawberry yogurt?\xe2\x80\x9d Subomi ribbed Nkechi. \xe2\x80\x9cYou better get home soon,\xe2\x80\x9d she warned him. \xe2\x80\x9cMomo is waiting for you.\xe2\x80\x9d

The next morning, Nkechi woke up earlier than usual, around 5 am. She called Subomi\xe2\x80\x99s nurse, and then his doctor, but neither picked up. She felt a surge of adrenaline. As if on autopilot, she put on a T-\xe2\x80\x89shirt and quietly drove to the hospital. When she got there, the doctor told her that Subomi had suffered a sudden embolism in his intestine. He was dead.

Nkechi\xe2\x80\x99s ears started to ring.

\xe2\x80\x9cIt can happen to anyone,\xe2\x80\x9d the doctor continued. The direct cause, in Subomi\xe2\x80\x99s case, remains impossible to ascertain. Blood disorders like SCD are straightforward risks for blood clots, which are the primary cause of embolisms\xe2\x80\x94but so are factors like age, certain medications, and high cholesterol. It\xe2\x80\x99s not the kind of thing that can be proved conclusively; someone with an AA genotype could have suffered the same fate.

The details didn\xe2\x80\x99t interest Nkechi. Her husband was dead. The doctor started expressing his sympathy, but she cut him off. She told her family, who wailed at the news, and then returned home to tell Momo, who, like his mother, was stoic. Her heart was racing and her internal monologue ran on an obsessive loop: \xe2\x80\x9cNkechi, what\xe2\x80\x99s your next move?\xe2\x80\x9d She was now a single mother, with a son who needed intensive medical supervision. Would she relocate somewhere\xe2\x80\x94\xe2\x80\x89maybe California, where her brother lived? Would she stay where she was? Would she continue their business? There had to be a funeral next week.

Subomi was buried in Ijebu-\xe2\x80\x89Ode, in front of friends who came from as far as Canada. Nkechi sleepwalked through it. None of her friends or family remember her crying.

Three months after his father died, Momo had a crisis: splenic sequestration. Nkechi noticed that he had suddenly become very quiet, and his stomach was unusually bloated. He stayed in the hospital overnight and received a blood transfusion. Some time later, Subomi\xe2\x80\x99s father called Nkechi to ask about the house he had given the couple as a wedding present; he was considering renting the place out, though he never did. In 2021, Momo had another crisis: sepsis. Its trigger was a bacterial infection of unknown provenance, and he spent ten days in the hospital on antibiotics. Nkechi\xe2\x80\x99s mother-\xe2\x80\x89in-\xe2\x80\x89law dropped in for a brief visit on the fifth day.

When Nkechi had married Subomi a decade prior, the bride price his family paid made her, on paper, a daughter of Ijebu-\xe2\x80\x89Ode. But few arrangements are permanent, and today, raising Momo is largely Nkechi\xe2\x80\x99s solo undertaking. In 2021, she withdrew Momo from his paternal grandmother\xe2\x80\x99s school and enrolled him in a different one two miles away. Nkechi and Momo now go to Mbaise for holidays. They rarely find occasion to test their status, by marriage and by birth, as daughter and son of Ijebu-\xe2\x80\x89Ode.

By the time I met Momo, shortly before his sixth birthday, the acute dramas of his diagnosis had subsided. Nkechi has told her son that he has \xe2\x80\x9cspecial blood.\xe2\x80\x9d Momo is about a meter tall\xe2\x80\x94\xe2\x80\x89a little small for his age, but not by much\xe2\x80\x94\xe2\x80\x89and his slightly distended stomach is visible under his butter-\xe2\x80\x89yellow school uniform. Nkechi lectures all his teachers about keeping him hydrated and avoiding intense sports. Momo isn\xe2\x80\x99t shy about his condition, even if he doesn\xe2\x80\x99t fully comprehend it. \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99ve been to the hospital like a hundred times!\xe2\x80\x9d he told me cheerfully. (Nkechi brings him to a government pediatric hospital every three months.) He and Nkechi have settled into their new equilibrium. I asked him about his last crisis, when he was on intravenous antibiotics. \xe2\x80\x9cI was on the drrrip!\xe2\x80\x9d he exclaimed, trilling his r\xe2\x80\x99s. Sometimes, Momo is the one to remind his mom to give him his pills, which he crunches like candy. Money can be tight, so Nkechi prioritizes Momo\xe2\x80\x99s medications and economizes elsewhere. As far as she knows, Momo is the only student in his kindergarten class with the disease. \xe2\x80\x9cBut some parents are weird about this,\xe2\x80\x9d she admitted. \xe2\x80\x9cSo who knows.\xe2\x80\x9d

Momo has a sunny temperament and speaks with the diction of someone who interacts mainly with adults. \xe2\x80\x9cI miss the old days,\xe2\x80\x9d he said one day, out of the blue, \xe2\x80\x9cwith my dad!\xe2\x80\x9d Nkechi raised an eyebrow. She\xe2\x80\x99s not convinced he actually remembers Subomi. Momo is an in-\xe2\x80\x89demand playmate at church and the ringleader of his many maternal cousins. When home alone, he\xe2\x80\x99s resourceful: a paper fan is a baseball bat, crumpled paper a soccer ball. One of his plastic toy soldiers is named Subomi.

The goal of SCD management, Dr. Akinyanju has written, is extending the \xe2\x80\x9csteady-\xe2\x80\x89state\xe2\x80\x9d period between crises. Momo has cleared the early-\xe2\x80\x89life danger zone for infections. But children with fewer resources can be far less lucky. In Lagos, I also met Tawakalitu Ayinde, a poor, unemployed single mother of four daughters, the last of whom has SCD. Nine-\xe2\x80\x89year-\xe2\x80\x89old Oluwaseyifunmi had seven strokes between 2017 and 2022; the last one, in January 2022, left one of her legs paralyzed and her speech garbled. She is still not on any regular medications.

As Momo gets older, malaria may trigger crises, but it\xe2\x80\x99s unlikely to be fatal. Viral hepatitis will remain a lifelong risk. He might reach puberty later than his peers. Recent research has suggested a genetic component to leg ulcers, which means Momo may be at higher risk for them, so he may start wearing compression bandages. And someday, he will have to accommodate the fact that stress, like the kind that comes from relationships or work, may trigger crises too. It all seemed far away, until I noticed Momo kissing a T. rex puppet over Christmas in Mbaise. \xe2\x80\x9cHold on,\xe2\x80\x9d he told me, \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99m doing something romantic.\xe2\x80\x9d

Even today, while Nkechi\xe2\x80\x99s friends and family almost universally disapprove of carrier marriages, they concede that Momo \xe2\x80\x9cdoesn\xe2\x80\x99t really look like a sickler.\xe2\x80\x9d During Nkechi\xe2\x80\x99s sister\xe2\x80\x99s wedding in Mbaise, a family friend, Sunny Opara, explained why he thought that \xe2\x80\x9ceducated people\xe2\x80\x9d shouldn\xe2\x80\x99t risk having SS children while Momo and his cousin streaked by. I pointed out that Momo\xe2\x80\x99s life chances were vastly better than those of previous generations with the disease, but Opara insisted that \xe2\x80\x9cprevention is better than the cure.\xe2\x80\x9d As Momo tore through the house, filled with four generations of people who loved him, I wondered what everyone would really prefer: a world in which genetically-\xe2\x80\x89at-\xe2\x80\x89risk Nkechi had made the supposedly responsible choice to break up with Subomi, or this one, in which she took a chance.

Someday, Momo might be a candidate for a bone-\xe2\x80\x89marrow transplant. The Sickle Cell Foundation Nigeria has just started evaluating patients for its first ever in-house transplant program, but so far, only a handful of well-\xe2\x80\x89to-\xe2\x80\x89do Nigerians have received the treatment, usually abroad, paying up to $500,000. Nkechi isn\xe2\x80\x99t counting on this. Throughout her life, her great strength, and great flaw, has been her laser focus on the present. She never wants Momo to feel the shame that racked her husband. Just three months after Subomi died, Nkechi started doing outreach again for the Sickle Cell Advocacy and Management Initiative. \xe2\x80\x9cI know he would have supported me speaking out,\xe2\x80\x9d she said. \xe2\x80\x9cThough maybe that\xe2\x80\x99s a low bar, because he supported everything I did.\xe2\x80\x9d

As Nkechi and Momo left church one Sunday, a male friend in the parking lot warned her about the effects of single motherhood on a young boy like Momo. \xe2\x80\x9cDon\xe2\x80\x99t bring him up as a woman!\xe2\x80\x9d he joked.

Momo stood up in the back seat and screamed, \xe2\x80\x9cI am strong!\xe2\x80\x9d

Nkechi snorted, and stepped on the gas. \xe2\x80\x9cWhat do they know?\xe2\x80\x9d
